title: What is a Convolution?
concept: A convolution slides a small learnable filter across an image, computing a weighted sum at each position — the same sliding-window math as Sobel, except a CNN learns the filter's numbers from data instead of a person hand-picking them.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In a Convolutional Neural Network, who determines the numbers inside the sliding filter?

options.text: A human engineer picks them by hand, like Sobel's numbers
They are always fixed at zero and never used

exercises.explain: Right — that's the whole shift Unit 96 set up. Now let's see exactly what that sliding operation is called.

cards.title: This is the moment
cards.body: Every sliding kernel from Phase 1 — blur, Sobel, edge detection — was building to this. You're about to see the exact same sliding-window operation, except now a neural network chooses the numbers itself. This is the 'aha' the whole classical CV arc was aimed at.

cards.title: What is a convolution?
cards.body: A convolution slides a small grid of numbers, called a filter or kernel, across an image. At every position, it multiplies the filter's numbers by the pixels underneath and adds the results into one number — a weighted sum, also called a dot product.

cards.title: A tiny flashlight scanning a photo
cards.body: Picture a small flashlight sliding row by row across a photograph. At each spot it doesn't just look — it multiplies what it sees by a fixed pattern of numbers and reports one score before creeping to the next spot.

cards.title: You already did this with Sobel
cards.body: Back in Unit 92, the Sobel filter slid a 3x3 grid of hand-picked numbers across an image to find edges. A convolution in a CNN does the identical sliding, multiplying, and summing — the only difference is where the numbers come from.

cards.title: The numbers aren't hand-picked anymore
cards.body: It's tempting to think a CNN's filters are designed by an engineer, the way Sobel's were. They're not — a CNN's filter starts as random numbers, and training nudges them until the filter learns to detect something useful, like an edge, a curve, or a texture.

cards.title: Sliding kernel: then vs. now
cards.body: Same sliding window, different source for the numbers:

left.text:
Fixed 3x3 kernel
numbers picked by an engineer

right.text:
Same 3x3 sliding window
numbers learned from data

exercises.prompt: What does a convolution compute at each position as its filter slides across an image?

options.text: A weighted sum (dot product) of the filter's numbers and the pixels underneath
The average brightness of the entire image
A random number unrelated to the pixels
The file size of that region

exercises.explain: Exactly — multiply matching pairs, then add them all up into a single number.

exercises.prompt: True or false: sliding a filter across an image and computing a weighted sum at each position is exactly what the Sobel filter from Unit 92 did.
exercises.explain: True. A convolution is that same sliding-window math — only the source of the filter's numbers changes.

exercises.sentence: A convolution slides a small filter across an image, multiplying and summing pixel values — but unlike Sobel, the filter's numbers are ___ during training.
exercises.explain: Learned is correct — that's the entire shift this phase is exploring.

exercises.prompt: Which of these describes what happens inside a CNN's convolution layer?

options.text: A small grid of learned numbers slides over the image, computing a dot product at each position
The image is resized to a smaller resolution
Pixels are sorted from brightest to darkest

exercises.explain: Right — that sliding dot product is the entire convolution operation.

exercises.prompt: You slide a 3x3 filter over a photo of a cat and compute a weighted sum at each position, just like Sobel did — except this filter's numbers came from training on thousands of images. What is this operation called, and why does it matter?

options.text: A convolution — it's the core operation that lets a CNN learn its own edge and texture detectors instead of a human designing them by hand
A rotation — it changes the orientation of the cat photo
A file compression step that shrinks the image size
A convolution — but it means the same thing as taking the image's histogram

exercises.explain: That's it! You just named the core operation of every CNN. Next: where those learned numbers actually come from, step by step.
